title: The Janitor Always Mops Twice

jokes.text: I suppose I should start at the end. My end, that is.
jokes.setup: Standard noir opening about starting at 'the end'
jokes.punchline: Clarifying he means his literal rear end/butt
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay that subverts noir conventions with scatological humor. Economical and well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I suppose I should start at the end. My end, that is.

jokes.text: That poor sap doing the two-step to nowhere is me.
jokes.setup: Noir narration describing himself in third person
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic description of having diarrhea as 'doing the two-step'
jokes.explanation: Creative euphemism for digestive distress that fits the noir pastiche perfectly. Good visual comedy implied.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: That poor sap doing the two-step to nowhere is me.

jokes.text: The victim? A handicap stall. The murderer? A butt. But whose butt?
jokes.setup: Treating bathroom mess like a crime scene investigation
jokes.punchline: Dramatically asking 'whose butt?' as if it's a murder mystery
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist comedy that treats mundane janitor work like a noir investigation. Perfect structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: The victim? A handicap stall. The murderer? A butt. But whose butt?

jokes.text: trouble walked in like it always does, on a great set of legs.
jokes.setup: Classic noir 'trouble walked in' setup
jokes.punchline: Camera reveals it's Dee, who definitely doesn't have 'a great set of legs'
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection that works because audience knows Dee. Visual reveal enhances the joke.

dialogue.line: trouble walked in like it always does, on a great set of legs.

jokes.text: Boy, has he got you trained. All that for a whistle? I wonder what you'd do for a biscuit.
jokes.setup: Watching Mac respond to Dennis blowing a whistle
jokes.punchline: Treating Mac like a dog who responds to commands for treats
jokes.explanation: Good character observation that treats Mac's subservience to Dennis like dog training. Well-structured escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Boy, has he got you trained. All that for a whistle? I wonder what you'd do for a biscuit.

jokes.text: Don't worry. In five minutes, he'll be up and chasing his tail again.
jokes.setup: Continuation of the dog metaphor after Mac gets knocked down
jokes.punchline: Predicting Mac will literally chase his tail like a dog
jokes.explanation: Solid callback that extends the dog metaphor. Not groundbreaking but consistent with the established joke.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Don't worry. In five minutes, he'll be up and chasing his tail again.

jokes.text: You know, they say any port in a storm, and this port just... Ah, shit, it's you!
jokes.setup: Charlie starting a smooth pickup line thinking he sees an attractive woman
jokes.punchline: Realizing mid-sentence it's Dee and immediately dropping the act
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect timing. The abrupt shift from smooth to disgusted is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You know, they say any port in a storm, and this port just... Ah, shit, it's you!

jokes.text: He's been diarrhea-poisoned.
jokes.setup: Dee explaining Frank's condition
jokes.punchline: Made-up term 'diarrhea-poisoned' that sounds medical but is nonsensical
jokes.explanation: Inventive fake medical terminology that's both absurd and memorable. Good economy of language.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: He's been diarrhea-poisoned.

jokes.text: He's absolutely carpet-bombed my bathroom. And my carpet.
jokes.setup: Describing the extent of Frank's digestive disaster
jokes.punchline: The mess spread beyond the bathroom to the actual carpet
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation joke with good visual imagery. The military metaphor works well for the gross-out humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: He's absolutely carpet-bombed my bathroom. And my carpet.

jokes.text: Huh. Lot of that going around.
jokes.setup: Dee's dramatic announcement about diarrhea poisoning
jokes.punchline: Charlie treating it like a common cold with casual dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response that treats the absurd as mundane

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Huh. Lot of that going around.

jokes.text: I'm a knockout blonde with a body that would make a statue blush.
jokes.setup: Dee defending herself against being called a goon
jokes.punchline: Wildly inaccurate self-description that contradicts reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Dee's delusional self-image is consistent and funny. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I'm a knockout blonde with a body that would make a statue blush.

jokes.text: I get more of a goon vibe from you.
jokes.setup: Dee's elaborate self-description
jokes.punchline: Charlie completely ignoring her claims and doubling down on the goon assessment
jokes.explanation: Effective deadpan response that deflates Dee's delusions. Good economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I get more of a goon vibe from you.

jokes.text: Make it stiff enough to stand on its head and sing 'Oh! Susanna'.
jokes.setup: Charlie ordering a drink in noir style
jokes.punchline: Overly elaborate and nonsensical description of how strong he wants his drink
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd Charlie-ism that combines noir tropes with his unique logic. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Make it stiff enough to stand on its head and sing 'Oh! Susanna'.

jokes.text: You can have tap.
jokes.setup: Charlie's elaborate drink request
jokes.punchline: Dee's completely unimpressed, practical response
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Charlie's pretensions. Maximum economy with strong contrast to his verbose request.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You can have tap.

jokes.text: The rat bastard liquefied my guts
jokes.setup: Quoting Frank's dramatic description of his condition
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic noir language for having diarrhea
jokes.explanation: Good use of noir language for gross bodily functions. Frank's characteristic dramatic overstatement.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: The rat bastard liquefied my guts

jokes.text: You're giving off big goon vibes, Dee. Very goonish.
jokes.setup: Continuing the goon theme despite Dee's protests
jokes.punchline: Charlie doubling down with 'Very goonish' as if it's a professional assessment
jokes.explanation: Solid continuation of the running gag. Charlie's commitment to the bit is funny even if not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You're giving off big goon vibes, Dee. Very goonish.

jokes.text: Don't be a liar, Frankie-Boy, or your pants will catch on fire. Or they would, if you were wearing any pants.
jokes.setup: Starting the childhood rhyme about liars' pants catching fire
jokes.punchline: Noting that Frank isn't wearing pants due to his bathroom emergency
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of a common saying with good observational humor about Frank's predicament.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Don't be a liar, Frankie-Boy, or your pants will catch on fire. Or they would, if you were wearing any pants.

jokes.text: I found enough beef to open a hamburger store.
jokes.setup: Charlie investigating who had problems with Frank
jokes.punchline: Using 'beef' to mean both grievances and hamburger meat
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay that fits Charlie's noir narration style. Good economy and fits the character's voice.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I found enough beef to open a hamburger store.

jokes.text: I'm a janitor. Cleaning up messes is what I do.
jokes.setup: Thug asking if Charlie thinks he can clean up this mess (meaning the situation)
jokes.punchline: Charlie taking it literally about his janitorial duties
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment where Charlie misses the metaphor and responds literally. Good integration of his job into the noir plot.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm a janitor. Cleaning up messes is what I do.

jokes.text: Her eyes glistened in the moonlight like fresh gasoline puddles.
jokes.setup: Typical noir description of a beautiful woman's eyes
jokes.punchline: Comparing them to gasoline puddles, which is both gross and weirdly specific to Charlie
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of noir romance tropes with Charlie's unique perspective. The gasoline comparison is perfectly off-putting and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Her eyes glistened in the moonlight like fresh gasoline puddles.

jokes.text: They were the kind of eyes you could fall in... and summer in. And winter in, too.
jokes.setup: Continuing the romantic description, starting with 'fall in love'
jokes.punchline: Taking 'fall in' literally and adding seasons as if her eyes are a vacation destination
jokes.explanation: Great example of Charlie's literal-mindedness derailing romantic clichés. Good structure with the misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: They were the kind of eyes you could fall in... and summer in. And winter in, too.

jokes.text: My boyfriend looks like a bad apple, but once you get to know Vince, you'd like him even less.
jokes.setup: Starting what sounds like a defense of her boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Revealing he gets worse upon closer inspection
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. The setup tricks you into expecting a defense, then delivers the opposite.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: My boyfriend looks like a bad apple, but once you get to know Vince, you'd like him even less.

jokes.text: You don't seem like the straightening type. Maybe 'cause you got me all twisted.
jokes.setup: Charlie offering to 'straighten out' her boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Playing with 'straight/twisted' as both literal and romantic metaphors
jokes.explanation: Competent wordplay that fits the noir dialogue style. Not exceptional but serviceable for the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: You don't seem like the straightening type. Maybe 'cause you got me all twisted.

jokes.text: Yeah, there's no booze, no legal cooze. Sounds like a snooze. And there's barely any dudes.
jokes.setup: Describing the disappointing party setup
jokes.punchline: Creating a rhyme scheme with increasingly absurd complaints
jokes.explanation: Simple rhyming joke that escalates Mac's complaints. The 'barely any dudes' adds a character-specific touch.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's no booze, no legal cooze. Sounds like a snooze. And there's barely any dudes.

jokes.text: Cricket? What are you doing here? Dee paid me to cover her shift, hence the getup.
jokes.setup: Charlie surprised to see Cricket at the bar
jokes.punchline: Cricket is wearing feminine clothing as a disguise, implying he's dressed as Dee
jokes.explanation: Solid visual gag that plays on Cricket's desperation and the absurdity of him impersonating Dee.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Cricket? What are you doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: Dee paid me to cover her shift, hence the getup.

jokes.text: More. Oh, yes. Do it. Harder. Yes. I'm a slut.
jokes.setup: Charlie asking what Cricket was doing in the basement
jokes.punchline: Cricket's overly sexual response to a simple question about his living situation
jokes.explanation: Classic Cricket moment of inappropriate oversharing. Strong character integration but relies on shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: More. Oh, yes. Do it. Harder. Yes. I'm a slut.

jokes.text: Frank hired me to go down to the local bars and stick my fingers in the garnish stations.
jokes.setup: Cricket explaining his 'honest living'
jokes.punchline: Revealing his job is contaminating food at bars
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy that reveals Frank's scheme while showing Cricket's moral degradation. Appropriately disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: Frank hired me to go down to the local bars and stick my fingers in the garnish stations.

jokes.text: When someone flashes a roll of nickels at you, you don't ask questions, you just do.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he took Frank's job
jokes.punchline: Treating a 'roll of nickels' like it's serious money worth compromising morals for
jokes.explanation: Perfect Cricket logic - treating pocket change like serious money. Good character-specific absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Cricket
dialogue.line: When someone flashes a roll of nickels at you, you don't ask questions, you just do.

jokes.text: Plus, I saw you put cat food in it.
jokes.setup: Charlie offering the Waitress a drink
jokes.punchline: She declined because she watched him put cat food in it
jokes.explanation: Classic Charlie moment revealing his questionable beverage preparation. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: Plus, I saw you put cat food in it.

jokes.text: Maybe I did, maybe I didn't. You did, Charlie, I saw you scoop it out of the can...
jokes.setup: Charlie trying to deny the cat food accusation
jokes.punchline: Waitress providing specific details of his cat food scooping
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the cat food bit with specific gross details. The back-and-forth works well.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Maybe I did, maybe I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: You did, Charlie, I saw you scoop it out of the can...

jokes.text: Why are you smiling? Well, I'm trying to look sexy. You're supposed to be dead.
jokes.setup: Charlie waking up to find Dee there
jokes.punchline: Dee's completely inappropriate response of trying to be sexy when she should be playing dead
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dee's vanity overriding the noir plot requirements. Her priorities are perfectly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Why are you smiling?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Well, I'm trying to look sexy.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You're supposed to be dead.

jokes.text: You're not supposed to be sexy. You're supposed to be the goon. I'm not the goddamn goon.
jokes.setup: Continuing argument about Dee's role in the scenario
jokes.punchline: Dee's frustrated insistence that she's not the goon despite all evidence
jokes.explanation: Solid continuation of the running gag. Dee's denial is consistent with her character's vanity.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You're not supposed to be sexy. You're supposed to be the goon.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I'm not the goddamn goon.

jokes.text: He gives us 30%, only Mac thinks it's ten.
jokes.setup: Dennis explaining their cut of Frank's scheme
jokes.punchline: Casually revealing he's been lying to Mac about their split
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis moment showing his casual manipulation of Mac. Good economy in revealing character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: He gives us 30%, only Mac thinks it's ten.

jokes.text: You talking to me? Yeah. Well, I can't tell 'cause I'm not looking at you. We ain't looking at you either.
jokes.setup: Classic tough guy confrontation line
jokes.punchline: Everyone admitting they're not making eye contact, defeating the intimidation
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of tough guy movie tropes with awkward honesty

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You talking to me? Yeah. Well, I can't tell 'cause I'm not looking at you.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: We ain't looking at you either.

jokes.text: We ain't looking at you either.
jokes.setup: Vince saying they're not looking at Charlie
jokes.punchline: Mac's childish response that makes no logical sense in context
jokes.explanation: Classic Mac non-sequitur that shows his lack of understanding. Simple but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: We ain't looking at you either.

jokes.text: I'm no matador, Frank, so you can cut the bull.
jokes.setup: Charlie confronting Frank about his story
jokes.punchline: Using matador/bull metaphor to call out Frank's lies
jokes.explanation: Solid noir-style wordplay that fits Charlie's character. Good economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm no matador, Frank, so you can cut the bull.

jokes.text: Red 40.
jokes.setup: Multiple mentions of Red 40 throughout the episode
jokes.punchline: Charlie using it as a tough guy one-liner before fighting
jokes.explanation: Good callback that turns food coloring into a noir one-liner

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Red 40.

jokes.text: Ammonia-bleach cocktail, aka the Janitor's Helper. Luckily, I'm immune. I've built up a healthy tolerance over the years, or unhealthy, depending on how you look at it.
jokes.setup: Charlie explaining his poison cocktail
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning he's immune due to years of chemical exposure
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy that perfectly integrates Charlie's job with the noir plot. The casual delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Ammonia-bleach cocktail, aka the Janitor's Helper. Luckily, I'm immune. I've built up a healthy tolerance over the years, or unhealthy, depending on how you look at it.

jokes.text: He's gonna wake up with his head pounding harder than a Chinese crossword puzzle. Huh? Was that... That one wasn't clear?
jokes.setup: Charlie trying to deliver a noir-style simile
jokes.punchline: Realizing his metaphor makes no sense and checking if it worked
jokes.explanation: Great meta moment where Charlie breaks character to check his own simile. The confusion is perfectly Charlie.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: He's gonna wake up with his head pounding harder than a Chinese crossword puzzle. Huh? Was that... That one wasn't clear?

jokes.text: No, you put bleach on poison, it cancels it out, I'm pretty sure. No, it makes the poison more poisoning.
jokes.setup: Charlie explaining his antidote logic
jokes.punchline: His completely wrong understanding of how poison and bleach interact
jokes.explanation: Perfect Charlie logic that's confidently wrong. His faith in his dangerous 'antidote' is very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: No, you put bleach on poison, it cancels it out, I'm pretty sure. No, it makes the poison more poisoning.

jokes.text: So that's why that shot I took wasn't vodka. It was bleach. The universal poison antidote.
jokes.setup: Charlie explaining his clever counter-plan
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong understanding of how antidotes work
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Charlie logic - hilariously wrong but delivered with complete confidence. Pure character comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: So that's why that shot I took wasn't vodka. It was bleach. The universal poison antidote.

jokes.text: I was undercover the whole time. I mean, what are you talking about? That doesn't even make sense.
jokes.setup: Dee suddenly claiming she was an undercover cop
jokes.punchline: The Waitress pointing out this makes no sense given everything we've seen
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment where Dee tries to retroactively justify her role. The Waitress's response grounds it in reality.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I was undercover the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: I mean, what are you talking about? That doesn't even make sense.

jokes.text: Now I'm about to shit my brains all over this floor. But don't worry. I'll mop twice. I always do.
jokes.setup: Charlie's final noir narration about his predicament
jokes.punchline: Promising to clean up his own mess, staying true to his janitor nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending that ties back to the title and Charlie's character. Dark but oddly heartwarming in its consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Now I'm about to shit my brains all over this floor. But don't worry. I'll mop twice. I always do.

tmdb_overview: In the black-and-white noir world of his memory, Charlie navigates the seedy underbelly of Philadelphia as Frank has been "diarrhea poisoned," and it's up to Charlie to clean up the mess.
